step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("post transcriptional modifications of RNA")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on reviewing the gathered evidence:
Option C - 3' polyadenylation involves adding a string of adenosines onto the 3' end of an mRNA molecule. This step happens exclusively in the cytoplasm after transcription completes.
Option B - 5' capping adds a 7-methyl-guanosine nucleotide unit to the beginning (5') of the nascent mRNA before export from the nucleus.
Option A - Splicing removes certain internal sections (introns) inside the initial RNA product, preserving just external stretches needed for eventual protein production once exported outside.
These steps happen to RNA transcripts after transcription ends and keep the message intact until translation begins in the cytoplasm.
However, Option D - Glycosylation, pertains strictly to covalently bonding sugar moieties to specific amino-acids in finished proteins rather than altering any RNA molecule itself.
Therefore I conclude glycosylation should never appear alongside those actual post-transcriptional RNA modifications, making my opinion align perfectly with earlier reasoning.
Final Answer:
raw_response:
Tool Call: retrieve("post transcriptional modifications of RNA")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on reviewing the gathered evidence:
Option C - 3' polyadenylation involves adding a string of adenosines onto the 3' end of an mRNA molecule. This step happens exclusively in the cytoplasm after transcription completes.
Option B - 5' capping adds a 7-methyl-guanosine nucleotide unit to the beginning (5') of the nascent mRNA before export from the nucleus.
Option A - Splicing removes certain internal sections (introns) inside the initial RNA product, preserving just external stretches needed for eventual protein production once exported outside.
These steps happen to RNA transcripts after transcription ends and keep the message intact until translation begins in the cytoplasm.
However, Option D - Glycosylation, pertains strictly to covalently bonding sugar moieties to specific amino-acids in finished proteins rather than altering any RNA molecule itself.
Therefore I conclude glycosylation should never appear alongside those actual post-transcriptional RNA modifications, making my opinion align perfectly with earlier reasoning.
Final Answer: \boxed{D} Yes, I agree with the previous conclusions provided by @agent1 and @agent2 regarding why glycosylation isn’t considered a post-transcriptional modification of RNA given that it acts solely on completed protein products. \boxed{\text{D}}
